opportunities.title: Structured Curriculum Resource Library to Accelerate Uplift Framework Adoption
opportunities.pain_point: The Uplift framework launched August 2025 across 80 schools and ~3,760 teaching staff with no published curriculum resource library, no confirmed external evaluation partner, and no dedicated EGM in post at launch (Patrick Ellis appointed September 2025). CSPD's 2023 Annual Report identifies NAPLAN numeracy gaps as a strategic priority ('imperative to increase transition from Strong to Exceeding'). The 2023 employee engagement data (69% score, 53% response rate) and self-identified 'communication gaps' signal prior difficulty with system-wide change adoption — a direct execution risk for Uplift. The absence of pre-built instructional materials means teachers carry the planning burden at precisely the moment they are being asked to change their practice.
opportunities.market_evidence: CECG's Catalyst program — the direct methodological ancestor of Uplift, run by CSPD's new EGM Patrick Ellis before he joined — produced 5,400+ curriculum resources for primary English and Mathematics in partnership with Ochre Education, saving teachers up to 6 hours per week. Deloitte Access Economics was engaged as external evaluator from Day 1 and published findings in 2024 showing 75% reading proficiency vs 66% national average and 13 of the top 20 ACT 'making the most difference' schools being CECG schools. MACS Vision for Instruction (2024, one year ahead of Uplift) showed early-adopter schools improving NAPLAN numeracy by up to 46 percentage points. BCE's AI-assisted lesson planning pilot saved 9.3 hours per teacher per week. The market-solutions pattern is clear: structured resources + external evaluation = credible outcomes within 3 years.
opportunities.opportunity: Engage CSPD to design and commission a structured curriculum resource library for the Uplift framework — covering primary English and Mathematics at minimum — using a co-development model with CSPD's internal Canvas team and subject matter experts. Simultaneously, structure an external evaluation engagement (modelled on CECG/Deloitte) that establishes a pre-Uplift NAPLAN baseline and measures outcomes at 12-, 24-, and 36-month intervals. Deliverables: resource standards framework, authoring workflow, initial resource set across K-6 priority subjects, and an evaluation protocol with data collection cadence. This directly reduces teacher cognitive load during transition and creates the credibility evidence CSPD will need for stakeholder reporting by 2027-2028.
opportunities.why_now: Patrick Ellis joined as EGM Learning Outcomes in September 2025 — he carries direct muscle memory from CECG's Catalyst build and will immediately recognise the resource gap. The first post-Uplift NAPLAN cycle will be conducted in 2026, creating a hard deadline for establishing a baseline. MACS's VFI is already producing early data (2025) that CSPD will be benchmarked against publicly. The window to establish an evaluation baseline before the first results cycle is narrow.

who_cares.buyer: Patrick Ellis, EGM Learning Outcomes — direct accountability for Uplift outcomes; Greg Elliott, EGM Quality & Performance — responsible for performance metrics and system improvement
who_cares.user: School principals and classroom teachers across 80 schools; the internal CSPD Canvas team who would co-develop and host resources

opportunities.confidence_reasoning: Pain confirmed by three sources (CSPD Annual Report NAPLAN admission, signals file int-risk-2, engagement score). CECG peer has validated the exact approach with measurable outcomes (75% reading proficiency, 6 hrs/week teacher saving). MACS corroborates at larger scale. Timing signal is hard-edged (2026 NAPLAN cycle, Patrick Ellis in post). The only gap is confirming CSPD does not already have a resource library in development — not confirmed in public sources but absence of announcement makes it unlikely.
sources: CSPD Annual Report 2023 p.22 (NAPLAN numeracy gap)
CSPD Annual Report 2023 p.38 (engagement score, communication gaps)

ai_capabilities.application: Authoring structured lesson plans and assessment items aligned to the Australian Curriculum and Uplift explicit instruction framework
Distilling CECG's 5,400+ Catalyst resources into adaptation templates calibrated to CSPD's school context and student demographics
Enabling teachers to query the resource library in natural language and receive Uplift-aligned lesson materials grounded in the canonical resource set
Mapping existing Canvas assessment data to a pre-Uplift NAPLAN baseline schema for the external evaluation protocol

opportunities.title: AI-Assisted Teacher Workload Reduction Programme Within the Domed Policy Framework
opportunities.pain_point: CSPD's 69% employee engagement score (53% response rate, 2023) identifies work-life balance as a key gap, and the NSW teacher shortage (ext-risk-2, score 0.60) is the highest-probability external risk to CSPD's operations. The Inter-Diocesan Teacher Workforce Working Group met five times in 2024 — a clear signal of urgency. The Uplift framework adds professional learning obligations to already-stretched teachers during its rollout phase. CSPD currently blocks all open generative AI ('domed' approach) and has no announced AI-for-teacher-workload programme. At a system of 3,760 teachers facing structured curriculum change, the compounding effect of pedagogical transformation + administrative burden + shortage market is a material retention risk.
opportunities.market_evidence: BCE deployed M365 Copilot to 12,500 educators — the world's largest K-12 generative AI rollout — and a trial at St Francis College Crestmead measured 9.3 hours saved per teacher per week. Eight pilot schools using AI-assisted teaching grew NAPLAN scores from 490 to 555 (2023 to 2024), with Year 3 improving 7.22% against a state average decline of 0.57%. BCE's custom Copilot Studio solution was grounded in Catholic values — directly showing that AI deployment within a values-aligned governance model is feasible, not in contradiction with CSPD's mission. CECG's 5,400+ structured curriculum resources achieved a parallel outcome through non-AI means (6 hrs/week saving), confirming teacher time relief is achievable within a Catholic system.
opportunities.opportunity: Design and pilot a CSPD-specific AI-for-teachers programme that operates entirely within the existing 'domed' governance model — targeting administrative tasks, lesson planning adaptation for Uplift, and formative assessment feedback generation rather than student-facing open AI. Deliverables: approved AI tool selection (M365 Copilot or equivalent Catholic-values-grounded alternative), a teacher pilot cohort design (modelled on BCE's St Francis trial), data collection protocol measuring hours saved and teacher sentiment, and a governance framework extending the 'domed' model to teacher-facing tools. The programme is distinct from student AI access — preserving CSPD's safeguarding posture while unlocking teacher-side productivity.
opportunities.why_now: BCE's Copilot outcomes (published August 2025) are now the benchmark the NSW Catholic sector will use when evaluating AI investment. The 2025 EA (99% approval) locked in 3% annual pay rises — adding ~$19m/year to the salary base — meaning retention is now financially critical, not just operationally. CSPD's teacher workforce planning window before the shortage deepens further (projected to 2030) makes 2026 the correct intervention point. The EGM IT Frances Waterford (elevated to EGM level in 2023) has private-sector ICT background and will understand the ROI framing.

who_cares.buyer: Frances Waterford, EGM Information Technology (CIO) — technology investment decisions; Daniel Lynch, EGM People & Culture — workforce retention and engagement strategy
who_cares.user: All 3,760 teaching staff; primarily class teachers in the Uplift implementation cohort

opportunities.confidence_reasoning: Pain confirmed by multiple independent sources (engagement score, teacher shortage data, EA cost signal). BCE peer has validated the approach with published quantitative outcomes in an Australian Catholic school context — the closest available analogue. The 'domed' AI policy creates a natural entry point (offer a within-policy solution rather than challenging the policy). The only gap is confirming CSPD's M365 licensing position and whether CSNSW has a diocesan-level Copilot arrangement already in negotiation — not confirmed in public sources.
sources: CSPD Annual Report 2023 p.38 (engagement, work-life balance)

opportunities.title: Principal-Facing Analytics Layer to Turn Canvas Data into System-Level Intelligence
opportunities.pain_point: Uplift is grounded in formative assessment, but CSPD has no confirmed system-level analytics platform translating Canvas assessment data into principal- or system-level insights. The scorecard rates 'Data Intelligence for School Improvement' as 'on-track' (not strong) specifically because 'the depth of analytics infrastructure is not visible from external evidence.' Canvas is deployed system-wide and generates rich assessment data — but without an analytics layer above it, that data stays inside individual classrooms. CSPD's former Director of Data Intelligence role was elevated to EGM level in 2023, signalling strategic intent — but no published technology roadmap or analytics infrastructure investment is documented. Principals cannot currently benchmark their schools against system averages or NAPLAN trends in real time.
opportunities.market_evidence: CEWA's LEADing Lights platform integrates student performance, attendance, wellbeing, HR, and finance data across 164 schools, providing real-time principal Power BI dashboards with NAPLAN and WACE benchmarking — aiming to move from 'big picture analytics' to 'moment in time, individual person recommendations.' CSNSW itself deployed an Azure Databricks + Power BI platform serving all 11 NSW dioceses, saving 6-8 weeks annually in data reconciliation — CSPD is already one of the 11 connected dioceses. This means a CSPD-specific analytics layer does not need to be built from scratch: it can be built on top of the CSNSW Azure infrastructure already in place, adding school-level and student-level pedagogical analytics on top of the census-compliance foundation.
opportunities.opportunity: Engage CSPD to design a school-improvement analytics layer — scoped to the Canvas LMS data already in production — that surfaces formative assessment trends, proficiency progression, and NAPLAN benchmark comparisons to principals and the EGM Learning Outcomes. Phase 1: diagnostic audit of what Canvas data is currently exported, what the CSNSW Azure Databricks platform already provides, and where the intelligence gap sits. Phase 2: principal dashboard design (Power BI or Canvas-native analytics) with system-average benchmarks and school-vs-system comparisons. Phase 3: Uplift implementation tracking — which schools are showing assessment improvement patterns consistent with framework adoption, giving Patrick Ellis an early signal of where to direct support.
opportunities.why_now: The first post-Uplift NAPLAN cycle in 2026 will generate data CSPD needs to interpret at system level. Without analytics infrastructure in place before that cycle, the results will be difficult to act on. The CSNSW Azure Databricks foundation (already serving CSPD) means the build cost is incremental, not greenfield. Frances Waterford's elevation to EGM IT in 2023 and private-sector ICT background make her a natural buyer for a capability that demonstrates measurable return on the technology investment.

who_cares.buyer: Frances Waterford, EGM Information Technology — technology platform and data infrastructure investment; Patrick Ellis, EGM Learning Outcomes — needs system-level implementation data to direct Uplift support resources
who_cares.user: 80 school principals (primary consumers of dashboards); the system analytics team under the EGM IT; Greg Elliott, EGM Quality & Performance

opportunities.confidence_reasoning: Pain confirmed by scorecard ('on-track' not 'strong' on data intelligence), market-solutions analysis (explicitly calls out the analytics gap), and the absence of any announced analytics platform in public sources. CEWA has validated the model at comparable scale (operational, principal dashboards working). CSNSW's Azure Databricks platform confirms the infrastructure foundation exists — making this an incremental build not a greenfield investment. Timing signal is hard (2026 NAPLAN cycle). The main gap is confirming CSPD's actual activation level on the CSNSW platform, which requires a discovery conversation.

opportunities.title: Operational Cost Efficiency Programme — Back-Office Systems Integration for the Sustainability Pillar
opportunities.pain_point: CSPD recorded a -$20.6m operating deficit in 2023, with salaries at 71.6% of expenditure. The 2025 EA locks in a further ~$19m per annum in salary cost increases — roughly matching the entire deficit — through 2026. Capital outflows ($85.9m in 2023) exceed capital revenues ($36.2m) by $49.7m annually. The 2024-2029 strategy's 'Sustainability' pillar is the stated response but no efficiency targets or system integration plans are documented publicly. CSPD runs at least three distinct platforms (Canvas, Classm8, Grace intranet) and almost certainly has fragmented finance and HR systems across 80+ schools — a pattern that at comparable scale consistently generates material avoidable overhead.
opportunities.market_evidence: MACS announced a SAP S/4HANA Cloud + SAP SuccessFactors ERP transformation via Infosys in July 2025 — explicitly framed as consolidating HR, finance, and operations across ~300 schools onto a single cloud platform, with 'AI-readiness' as an explicit goal. CEWA resolved the same fragmentation post-2020 governance restructure using Freshservice for ITSM and Microsoft cloud for data management, with schools subsequently requesting access to the centralised tooling. CSNSW's Azure Databricks platform already saves 6-8 weeks per diocese annually in compliance reporting — a proof point that system-level integration reduces overhead even at the reporting layer.
opportunities.opportunity: Conduct a back-office systems diagnostic across CSPD's finance, HR, facilities, and IT service management functions — mapping current platform fragmentation, manual process overhead, and integration gaps. Produce a prioritised efficiency roadmap tied to the Sustainability pillar, with identified cost reduction or cost avoidance opportunities quantified. Likely scope areas: HR/payroll integration (particularly relevant given EA complexity and payroll escalation), IT service management consolidation (CEWA Freshservice model), and finance system rationalisation. This is a discovery-to-roadmap engagement, not a system implementation — scoped to identify the efficiency opportunity before a capital investment decision.
opportunities.why_now: The 2025 EA has crystallised the forward salary cost commitment — the financial pressure is now locked in and quantifiable. The governance restructure (company limited by guarantee from January 2023) creates a clean organisational mandate for professional back-office operations analogous to the private sector — different from the pre-2023 Diocesan Schools Council model. Gerard Giesekam (EGM Finance & Infrastructure) is CSPD's representative on the CSNSW Finance Working Group — indicating engagement with sector-wide financial management innovation. MACS's ERP announcement (July 2025) puts the question on the sector agenda.

who_cares.buyer: Gerard Giesekam, EGM Finance & Infrastructure — P&L accountability and capital programme oversight; Jack de Groot, CEO — Sustainability pillar delivery
who_cares.user: System finance and HR teams; school principals and business managers dealing with administrative overhead

opportunities.confidence_reasoning: Pain confirmed by the operating deficit (hard financial data), the EA salary escalation (locked in), and the multi-platform technology profile (evidenced but not fully mapped). MACS has validated the ERP approach at comparable scale but outcome data is not yet published (announced July 2025, implementation ongoing). CEWA validates the ITSM component with qualitative outcomes. The specific fragmentation severity at CSPD is unknown — finance and HR system state is not visible in public sources. Confidence is medium because the pain is real but the efficiency quantum is unconfirmed without a diagnostic.
sources: CSPD Annual Report 2023 p.44-45 (operating deficit, salary costs)

opportunities.title: LBOTE Student Support Framework — Uplift Adaptation for EAL/D Learners at Scale
opportunities.pain_point: CSPD has 53.4% LBOTE students — materially above the NSW Catholic sector average of 36% and the highest proportion among the researched peer systems. No peer has published a validated system-level EAL/D support model at comparable demographic concentration. The Uplift framework (explicit instruction, structured literacy) is founded on evidence-based methods that generally support EAL/D learners, but adaptation for non-English-speaking home students is not documented in any available CSPD source. The online assessment cost-recovery transition from July 2025 adds cost burden specifically to schools disproportionately serving LBOTE students who rely on diagnostic assessments. Low-to-mid fee Islamic and evangelical Christian schools in western Sydney are explicitly targeting LBOTE families — CSPD's most distinctive demographic is also its most contested segment.
opportunities.market_evidence: The market-solutions file explicitly identifies this as a gap: 'No peer has published a credible solution to the LBOTE student support challenge at scale. This is CSPD's most distinctive structural characteristic and no market solution exists that has been validated at comparable scale.' This is an unaddressed design problem — which is simultaneously a risk (no validated model to copy) and a differentiation opportunity (CSPD could be the system that develops and publishes the model, strengthening its community positioning against faith-based competitors). BCE's AI-assisted learning pilot and CECG's structured curriculum resources provide building blocks that could be adapted for EAL/D contexts.
opportunities.opportunity: Design an EAL/D-specific adaptation of the Uplift curriculum resources and professional learning framework — identifying where structured literacy and explicit instruction approaches require modification for students with English as an additional language. Scope: diagnostic review of current EAL/D resourcing across CSPD schools; mapping of Uplift materials against EAL/D pedagogy evidence base; co-design with CSPD's Wellbeing Directorate and community engagement leads; production of adapted instructional resources for the highest-prevalence language communities. Position the outputs as a publishable CSPD innovation — creating reputational value with LBOTE families and differentiating from competitors who lack the demographic insight to develop this.
opportunities.why_now: Low-to-mid fee independent schools are growing at 3.3%/year in CSPD's catchment, specifically targeting LBOTE families. The Uplift framework launch (August 2025) creates a natural integration point — the adaptation work must happen before the resource library is finalised to avoid expensive retrofitting. The 2026 Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander fee waiver (Innovate RAP) signals CSPD's willingness to make material equity commitments. Dianne Van Berlo (EGM Student Support) is the natural executive lead.

who_cares.buyer: Dianne Van Berlo, EGM Student Support — LBOTE and equity student support; Patrick Ellis, EGM Learning Outcomes — Uplift resource quality
who_cares.user: EAL/D specialist teachers and class teachers across CSPD's highest-LBOTE schools; school principals in Blacktown, Parramatta, and Cumberland LGAs

opportunities.confidence_reasoning: Pain is evidenced by structural demographic data (53.4% LBOTE — hard ACARA figure) and competitor threat (independent schools growing at 3.3%/year targeting this cohort). However, no peer has validated a solution at this scale, meaning there is no direct case study to cite. The opportunity is real and strategically significant, but confidence is medium because the solution requires original design work rather than adaptation of a proven peer model — execution risk is higher and CSPD's current internal EAL/D capability is not documented in public sources.

opportunities.title: Parent Communication and Reporting Innovation — Activating Canvas Data for Richer Family Engagement
opportunities.pain_point: CSPD has Canvas LMS deployed system-wide with parent-facing visibility features and six years of TTFM longitudinal survey data — the infrastructure for richer, more frequent parent communication exists. However, no reporting innovation or parent communication programme is documented for CSPD. The Five-Year Strategic Plan's 'Relevance' pillar explicitly prioritises student and family experience, but no specific programme addresses this at the reporting and communication level. The 'domed' approach to technology means parents receive communications within CSPD-mediated channels — raising the stakes for making those channels substantive.
opportunities.market_evidence: SCS announced a school reporting overhaul in February 2026, with its executive director declaring parents 'short-changed' by current twice-yearly academic reports; a new framework assessing creativity, discernment, resilience, practical wisdom, and problem-solving alongside academic results is under development with Boston College researchers. This directly signals that parent reporting is becoming a sector-wide differentiator and competitive battleground. SCS is 12+ months ahead on this agenda. CSPD has Canvas already deployed — the question is whether it is being used to its communication potential or just as a gradebook and LMS.
opportunities.opportunity: Design a CSPD parent communication programme built on the Canvas foundation: (1) audit current Canvas parent-facing feature activation across all 80 schools, identifying schools where parental visibility is underutilised; (2) develop a communication framework that goes beyond grades to include learning progress indicators aligned with the Uplift framework (explicit instruction mastery, formative assessment progress); (3) pilot enhanced reporting at 5-10 schools before system-wide rollout. This is not a technology procurement — it is a programme design and change management engagement, leveraging infrastructure already paid for.
opportunities.why_now: SCS's February 2026 announcement creates a competitive timing signal — the window to match the sector before SCS's new framework is live is roughly 12-18 months. The Uplift framework launch (August 2025) creates a natural new communication narrative: parents can be told what Uplift means for their child's learning, and progress within the framework can be communicated through Canvas. The 'Relevance' pillar is newly active, creating organisational mandate.

who_cares.buyer: Elizabeth Scully, EGM Communications & Engagement — community and parent communication; Jack de Groot, CEO — Relevance pillar of Five-Year Strategic Plan
who_cares.user: School principals and class teachers (who produce reports); parents across CSPD's 49,577-student cohort

opportunities.confidence_reasoning: Pain is inferred from competitive gap (SCS announced a programme; CSPD has not) and the coverage check against the Relevance pillar — not from explicit CSPD self-identification of this as a problem. Canvas deployment is confirmed system-wide but current activation level is unknown. SCS's programme is still in development (no outcome data). Confidence is medium because the competitive signal is real and the infrastructure enabler exists, but direct evidence of CSPD experiencing parent communication dissatisfaction is not available in public sources.

opportunities.title: System-to-School Communication and Leadership Visibility Programme for Strategic Initiative Adoption
opportunities.pain_point: The 2023 Annual Report explicitly identifies 'need for greater System Leader visibility' and 'better communication' as staff feedback themes (p.38). CSPD is simultaneously implementing the Uplift framework, the Five-Year Strategic Plan, a new governance model, the Wellbeing Directorate, and multiple capital projects — all across 80 schools with a 69% engagement score and 47% non-response rate. The new company limited by guarantee structure, while governance-sound, increases perceived distance between the ELT and individual school communities. Initiative fatigue at the school level is a real risk when multiple system-level programmes arrive simultaneously without clear sequencing or two-way dialogue structures.
opportunities.market_evidence: SCS's school reporting overhaul (February 2026) is in part a communication investment — the framing ('parents short-changed') signals that school-community communication quality is now a competitive variable, not just an internal HR matter. CECG's Catalyst programme explicitly invested in teacher communities of practice and professional learning infrastructure — the market-solutions pattern consistently shows that successful system-wide pedagogical reform requires structured communication feedback loops (principal forums, school improvement networks, structured principal coaching) not just top-down broadcast.
opportunities.opportunity: Design a system-to-school communication architecture for the Uplift implementation period: (1) structured principal community of practice (monthly, chaired by Patrick Ellis and Lisa Crampton) with a documented feedback loop to the ELT; (2) school implementation health check framework — a lightweight self-assessment principals complete quarterly, surfacing barriers before they become rollout failures; (3) Grace intranet content and cadence review to determine whether it supports two-way exchange or is purely broadcast. This is a change management and communication design engagement, not a technology build. Deliverable is a communication protocol and governance structure, not a platform.
opportunities.why_now: Uplift is in its first full implementation year (2026). The window to establish communication cadences before implementation fragmentation sets in is now. Patrick Ellis and Greg Elliott are both newly in post — they have the organisational appetite to shape how the system communicates about learning outcomes. The 99% EA approval (October 2025) indicates staff trust in leadership, creating a favourable climate for structured engagement.

who_cares.buyer: Patrick Ellis, EGM Learning Outcomes — needs a feedback mechanism to assess Uplift adoption; Jack de Groot, CEO — leader visibility is a personal accountability item from 2023 staff feedback
who_cares.user: 80 school principals; system EGMs; school leadership teams managing change adoption

opportunities.confidence_reasoning: Pain confirmed by explicit self-identification in the 2023 Annual Report (unusual candour — this is not inferred). CECG's success with Catalyst is partly attributable to professional learning community infrastructure. However, no peer has published a specific case study on system-to-school communication architecture as a distinct programme — the evidence is pattern-based rather than a single validated solution. Confidence is medium because the pain is confirmed but the solution design is less precedented than resource library or analytics approaches.

opportunities.title: Enrolment Demand Conversion Strategy — Retaining Families Turned Away During Capacity Constraints
opportunities.pain_point: More than 500 families are turned away annually from Westmead Catholic schools alone — these are families actively seeking CSPD enrolment who are forced to alternatives. With the new Sacred Heart Primary Westmead campus opening Term 3 2026 and CBGA capital grants funding the Schofields corridor, CSPD is adding capacity — but if turned-away families have permanently enrolled elsewhere, the capacity will not fill to potential. CSPD's long-run enrolment CAGR of 0.71% (2008-2025) underperforms the NSW Catholic sector rate of ~1.25%, partly reflecting this structural demand leakage. Low-to-mid fee independent schools growing at 3.3%/year are actively winning families CSPD cannot accommodate, and once enrolled, families rarely transfer.
opportunities.market_evidence: No peer in this study has published a demand conversion or re-engagement model for turned-away families. This is an identified market gap. The competitor data is instructive: independent schools' advantage is partly 'responsiveness to parent preferences' — suggesting that the manner in which CSPD communicates with waitlisted families (or doesn't) is a differentiator. CSPD's Canvas deployment with parent-facing communication features provides an infrastructure channel, but there is no evidence it is being used for prospective family engagement before enrolment.
opportunities.opportunity: Design a waitlist engagement and demand retention programme: (1) a structured communication sequence for families on capacity waitlists, including regular updates on construction timelines, confirmation of priority placement when capacity opens, and interim engagement with the CSPD community (school events, parish connections); (2) a data capture process to track which waitlisted families enrol elsewhere vs. return when places become available; (3) coordination with the Elizabeth Scully (EGM Communications & Engagement) team to ensure the Westmead campus opening is treated as a re-enrolment marketing event, not just a construction milestone. This is a CRM-light engagement design, not a technology platform build.
opportunities.why_now: Sacred Heart Primary Westmead opens Term 3 2026 — the immediate window for the Westmead corridor families. The Schofields CBGA capital grant projects are also in delivery. If CSPD does not have a waitlist re-engagement programme in place before Term 1 2026 enrolments close, the new campus will open into a market where turned-away families have already settled at alternatives. The 2025 Innovate RAP and free fees for First Nations families from 2026 create additional community engagement touchpoints in the same corridor.

who_cares.buyer: Elizabeth Scully, EGM Communications & Engagement — parent and community relations; Jack de Groot, CEO — enrolment growth is a strategic and financial priority
who_cares.user: School principals and enrolment officers at capacity-constrained schools; families on current waitlists

opportunities.confidence_reasoning: The excess demand (500+ turned away) is confirmed by public source data. The enrolment leakage risk is logically sound. However, no peer has validated a demand retention model in this context, and CSPD's specific waitlist management processes are not documented in public sources. The opportunity is plausible and timely, but it rests on an inference about what CSPD is not doing rather than confirmed evidence of a gap — lowering confidence to low. Confidence would increase with a single discovery conversation confirming current waitlist management practice.

Scoring waitlisted families by likelihood of re-engagement based on tenure, communication response patterns, and proximity to new capacity sites

cecg.name: Catholic Education Canberra and Goulburn
cecg.framing_note: CECG is an evidence-leader. These opportunities are framed for what comes AFTER Catalyst Phase 1 — deepening, extending, and compounding what CECG has already built. No opportunity here positions CECG as lacking basic practices. The question throughout is: what does the next frontier look like for the system that wrote the book?

opportunities.title: Generative AI Teacher Productivity Layer Grounded in the Catalyst Framework
opportunities.pain_point: CECG discussed AI in classrooms at the 2025 System Day but has not announced a platform, policy, or deployment. The signals file classifies AI readiness as 'watch' — CECG is 1–2 years behind peer leaders. Brisbane Catholic Education has already deployed Microsoft 365 Copilot to 12,500 educators (the largest K-12 Copilot rollout globally), measuring 9.3 hours saved per teacher per week at a pilot school and growing NAPLAN scores from 490 to 555 (2023–2024) in AI-assisted schools while the state average fell 0.57%. CECG's ICT investment is rising (cited as a 2026 fee driver) but without a named strategic direction. Critically, CECG already saves teachers up to 6 hours per week through structured curriculum resources — a generative AI layer is the logical next compounding productivity step, not a replacement for Catalyst's structured approach.
opportunities.market_evidence: BCE deployed M365 Copilot system-wide with measured outcomes: 9.3 hours/week saved per teacher (St Francis College trial), NAPLAN Year 3 up 7.22% vs state average decline of 0.57% in AI-assisted schools (CathNews, August 2025). BCE built a custom Copilot Studio chatbot grounded in Catholic values — demonstrating values-aligned AI deployment is feasible. MACS's July 2025 SAP ERP investment explicitly frames AI readiness as a goal ('paves the way to adopt generative AI applications geared towards educational outcomes'). CEWA has 90,000+ Microsoft 365 users across 164 schools as its AI-ready infrastructure foundation. No Catholic system of CECG's scale has yet failed with this approach — the question is sequencing, not risk.
opportunities.opportunity: Design and pilot a teacher-facing AI productivity programme for CECG that is explicitly positioned as a Catalyst extension, not a departure from it. Phase 1: AI tool selection and governance framework scoped to teacher-side tasks (lesson planning adaptation within the Catalyst HITP framework, formative assessment feedback drafting, communication and reporting). Phase 2: pilot cohort of 3–5 schools measuring hours saved, teacher sentiment, and any assessment signal. Phase 3: system-wide rollout with Catholic-values governance (modelled on BCE's Copilot Studio approach). The framing to CECG: this compounds the 6 hours/week Catalyst already saves — the two are additive, not competing. The new ED's background at ACARA (data systems, national assessment) and emphasis on procurement efficiency makes this a natural fit for his priorities.
opportunities.why_now: BCE's results were published publicly in August 2025 — this is now the benchmark every Australian Catholic system is measured against. CECG's 2025 System Day explicitly discussed 'where technology and AI fit into modern classrooms' (signals.json), signalling internal readiness for a proposal. New ED David de Carvalho commenced mid-2025 with fresh strategic priorities and a background at ACARA (national data systems) — the first 12–18 months is the window to influence his technology investment agenda before ICT budget cycles lock in. MACS's ERP transformation (announced July 2025) signals peers are accelerating AI-readiness investment now.

who_cares.buyer: David de Carvalho, Executive Director — new strategic mandate, emphasis on procurement efficiency and measurable outcomes; Patrick Ellis, Education Lead — Catalyst programme custodian, needs AI to deepen Catalyst not compete with it
who_cares.user: All 1,986 FTE teaching staff, primarily class teachers already trained in HITP who would use AI tools within the Catalyst instructional framework

opportunities.confidence_reasoning: Pain confirmed by multiple independent sources: signals.json (AI watch status), scorecard.json (AI readiness watch), profile.json (ICT rising cost without strategic direction), market-solutions.json (CECG explicitly identified as 1–2 years behind BCE). BCE has published quantitative outcomes in a directly comparable Australian Catholic school context. Timing signals are hard-edged: 2025 System Day discussion, new ED in post, BCE benchmark now public. The main gap is confirming CECG's current M365 licensing level and whether any internal AI working group has already selected a platform — not confirmed in public sources.

ai_capabilities.application: AI drafts lesson plan variations, formative assessment feedback, and parent communication within CECG's Catalyst HITP instructional framework — teachers edit and approve rather than write from scratch.

AI retrieves relevant sections from the 5,400+ Ochre Education curriculum resources and synthesises a targeted answer to a teacher's planning question, grounded in CECG's own resource library rather than the open internet.

An agent handles multi-step teacher admin workflows — pulling assessment results, formatting progress reports, and drafting communication to families — so teachers spend the recovered time on instruction rather than documentation.

opportunities.title: System-Level Analytics Layer to Turn Catalyst Assessment Data into Principal Intelligence
opportunities.pain_point: CECG has built best-in-sector assessment data collection infrastructure: DIBELS, PAT-R, PAT-M, Phonics Screening Check, NAPLAN, and Annual Evidence-based Improvement Planning Meetings at 100% school participation (scorecard.json rates this 'strong'). What the scorecard does not rate as strong is the analytics delivery layer — there is no publicly described platform connecting this data to real-time principal decision-making or system-level benchmarking across 59 schools. The market-solutions file explicitly flags this: 'The technology platform enabling real-time analytics for 59 schools is not publicly described.' New ED David de Carvalho is described as 'deeply quantitative' (former ACARA CEO, led the national assessment authority) — the absence of a unified analytics platform is misaligned with his known operating style and stated data emphasis.
opportunities.market_evidence: CEWA's LEADing Lights platform integrates student performance, attendance, graduation rates, and wellbeing data benchmarked against school, system, and NAPLAN averages in real time for all principals across 164 schools — described as enabling principals to 'investigate the impact of new initiatives via live analytics' (market-solutions.json). Outcome is rated 'mixed' (platform works, cultural adoption is the harder problem). CSNSW deployed an Azure Databricks + Power BI platform serving all 11 NSW dioceses, saving 6–8 weeks annually in data reconciliation — CECG's NSW schools may already be partially connected to this infrastructure. MACS's July 2025 SAP ERP investment similarly cites unified data infrastructure as an AI-readiness prerequisite.
opportunities.opportunity: Engage CECG to design a school-improvement analytics layer above their existing assessment data collection — connecting DIBELS, PAT, and NAPLAN results to a principal-facing dashboard that benchmarks each school against system averages and flags early-warning patterns (students below expected trajectory, schools diverging from Catalyst adoption metrics). Phase 1: diagnostic audit of what data currently flows from assessment tools into what systems, and whether CECG's NSW schools participate in the CSNSW Azure Databricks platform. Phase 2: principal dashboard design — system-average benchmarks, school-vs-system comparisons, year-on-year Catalyst adoption tracking. Phase 3: ED/system-level view for central office: network-wide pattern detection, identification of which schools need coaching support, and longitudinal outcome tracking for the summative Deloitte evaluation. The pitch to de Carvalho: this is the infrastructure ACARA builds nationally — CECG can have the equivalent for its own 59 schools.
opportunities.why_now: De Carvalho commenced mid-2025 with explicitly data-oriented leadership priorities. The Deloitte formative evaluation ended March 2024 — any summative evaluation will require the infrastructure to produce clean longitudinal data. CECG is entering a Catalyst Phase 2 deepening cycle; without analytics visibility the system cannot know which schools are sustaining progress and which are regressing. The MACS and CEWA peer investments (both publicly announced in 2025) signal this is the investment wave peers are executing now.

who_cares.buyer: David de Carvalho, Executive Director — former ACARA CEO, will immediately recognise the national assessment infrastructure analogy; Patrick Ellis, Education Lead — needs system-level data to direct coaching resources efficiently
who_cares.user: 59 school principals (primary dashboard consumers); CECG central office analytics or IT team; Deloitte (if a summative evaluation is recommissioned)

opportunities.confidence_reasoning: Pain confirmed by scorecard (assessment collection strong, analytics delivery not rated), market-solutions.json (explicit flag of analytics gap and de Carvalho data orientation), and profile.json (technology/data system contracts listed as a known gap). CEWA has validated the principal analytics model at comparable scale. De Carvalho's background (ACARA CEO) is a strong a priori signal that analytics infrastructure will be a personal priority. Timing signal exists (new ED in post, Deloitte evaluation ended, need for summative evidence). The main unknown is current SIS and data infrastructure specifics — confirming those requires a discovery conversation.

ai_capabilities.application: The analytics layer models which students are at risk of falling below proficiency thresholds based on early DIBELS and PAT trajectory data, surfacing early-warning flags to principals before a student reaches crisis point.

Assessment data from DIBELS, PAT-R, PAT-M, and Phonics Screening Check — currently collected in separate systems — is normalised into a unified schema so all 59 schools can be compared on consistent dimensions in a single principal dashboard.

Principals query the analytics platform in natural language ('How does my Year 2 reading performance compare to the system average this term?') and receive accurate answers drawn from the aggregated assessment data.

opportunities.title: Catalyst Sustainability Infrastructure — Structured Onboarding and Coaching at Scale for New Teachers
opportunities.pain_point: Catalyst's Phase 1 adoption metrics are exceptional: 88.6% of 1,500+ teachers use HITP daily, 92.4% believe it merits continued focus (2025 System Day survey). But the same survey reveals 16.7% would not sustain HITP use beyond formal requirements — one in six teachers is compliance-driven, not intrinsically motivated. With 90%+ retention implying ~200 teacher departures per year, approximately 200 new teachers must be inducted into Catalyst annually without a publicly described structured onboarding pathway. Patrick Ellis — the Catalyst programme architect — has moved to CSPD. The Deloitte evaluation is formative only; no summative evidence yet proves gains are self-sustaining without continued central resourcing. This is not a problem unique to CECG: every system that has achieved strong initial adoption faces the same sustainability inflection.
opportunities.market_evidence: CSPD's Uplift programme (launched August 2025, modelled on Catalyst by Patrick Ellis) faces an earlier version of this exact problem — no published onboarding pathway for new staff has been confirmed. MACS's Vision for Instruction (launched 2024, 4 years behind Catalyst) is building its induction infrastructure now — NAPLAN early results show improvements at adopter schools but MACS has not yet published any teacher onboarding or sustainability model. Catholic Education Tasmania's Insight into Learning programme (PAT data showing 11% outperformance vs non-participating schools) has sustained two years post-adoption, providing early evidence that the model can hold. The market has not yet produced a purpose-built AI-assisted HITP coaching tool — CECG is positioned to build the reference implementation.
opportunities.opportunity: Design an AI-assisted teacher onboarding and coaching system for Catalyst sustainability — specifically targeting: (1) new teacher induction into HITP (replacing the informal mentoring that currently scales poorly), (2) ongoing coaching nudges for the 16.7% who are compliance-driven rather than intrinsically motivated, and (3) differentiated support for rural Goulburn diocese teachers who cannot easily access in-person coaching from Shaping Minds. Deliverables: a structured HITP digital learning pathway (micro-credential format, mapped to CECG's own HITP standards); an AI coaching assistant that gives teachers in-the-moment feedback on lesson structure aligned to Catalyst principles; an analytics view tracking individual teacher adoption trajectories over time. Framing: this is not remediation — it is institutionalising the programme so Catalyst can survive any individual departure, including the new ED, and scale to any new school that joins the system.
opportunities.why_now: Patrick Ellis's departure to CSPD is the most visible manifestation of the key-person risk (signals.json, int-r2). The concurrent ED transition (Fox to Betts to de Carvalho) removed the second named champion. CECG is at the exact moment — high adoption, institutional memory partially dispersed — where sustainability infrastructure either gets built or the programme begins to regress. De Carvalho's faith-formation framing of Catalyst (rather than pure outcomes data) may actually accelerate this: a coaching system aligned to the 'Pilgrims of Hope' and transcendentals framing of the 2025 System Day is consistent with his stated priorities.

who_cares.buyer: Patrick Ellis, Education Lead — directly accountable for Catalyst continuity and the most likely internal champion post-Fox; David de Carvalho, Executive Director — programme sustainability is a governance and risk question for the new board structure
who_cares.user: New teachers joining CECG each year (~200 per annum based on 10% attrition of 1,986 FTE); the 16.7% of existing teachers who are compliance-driven; Shaping Minds coaches (as a tool complement, not replacement)

opportunities.confidence_reasoning: Pain confirmed by multiple independent sources: the 16.7% compliance-driver figure from the 2025 System Day survey itself, the Deloitte formative evaluation (summative pending), the documented Patrick Ellis departure, and the annual attrition mathematics (~200 teachers/year requiring induction). The pain is CECG-specific and data-confirmed, not inferred. No peer has published a purpose-built AI solution to this exact problem — which positions this as a first-mover opportunity rather than a catch-up play. The framing aligns with both the new ED's governance priorities and the Education Lead's programme stewardship responsibilities.

ai_capabilities.application: A conversational coaching agent walks new teachers through HITP lesson design step by step, answering questions about Catalyst principles and offering real-time feedback on their lesson structure — available 24/7 without requiring a Shaping Minds visit.

The system identifies teachers showing early compliance-driven adoption patterns (using HITP only when observed, not consistently) and flags them to the Education Lead for targeted coaching before disengagement becomes entrenched.

The onboarding pathway generates personalised HITP practice scenarios for each new teacher based on their year level, subject domain, and school context — ensuring rural Goulburn diocese teachers receive content as relevant as ACT metro teachers.

The system synthesises each teacher's coaching interaction history and assessment progression into a concise quarterly coaching summary for the Education Lead, replacing manual tracking across hundreds of individual teacher journeys.

opportunities.title: Catalyst Phase 2 — Extending the Evidence-Based Framework to Secondary and Writing Domains
opportunities.pain_point: Catalyst's validated outcomes are concentrated in primary literacy (K–6 reading: DIBELS, Phonics Screening Check, proficient readers 75% vs 66% national) and early mathematics (K–6 Mathematics curriculum resources, PAT-M). There is no public evidence of a validated Catalyst framework for secondary English or Mathematics, for writing as a distinct domain (separate from reading), or for STEM. The 2025 System Day featured Natalie Wexler (a writing instruction specialist), signalling internal awareness of the writing gap — but no programme has been announced. CECG's secondary schools (Year 7–12 colleges) generate the highest-value family decisions, are the site of the Year 6–7 transition leakage to independent schools, and are the level where Canberra Grammar, CGGS (IB Diploma), and Radford directly compete.
opportunities.market_evidence: MACS's Vision for Instruction (2024) focused on primary literacy and mathematics — the same Phase 1 scope as Catalyst — and published early NAPLAN results. MACS has not yet published a secondary extension or writing framework, meaning no peer has cracked this problem at scale. CECG's invitation of Natalie Wexler (whose Knowledge in Colour framework addresses writing as knowledge-building) for a system workshop in November 2025 (co-hosted with Think Forward Educators) is the closest available signal of where CECG is looking for Phase 2. BCE's AI-assisted teaching pilot (490 to 555 NAPLAN points at pilot schools) spanned primary years — no secondary extension data published. The sector gap is confirmed: no Australian Catholic system has published a secondary or writing extension to an explicit instruction primary framework.
opportunities.opportunity: Partner with CECG to design the Catalyst Phase 2 scope — focusing on two domains where evidence base exists and CECG has already signalled interest: (1) evidence-based writing instruction (Knowledge in Colour / Wexler framework integrated into Catalyst's HITP structure) and (2) secondary explicit instruction extension (Years 7–10 initially, where transition retention is at risk). Deliverables: Phase 2 scope document and resource audit; writing instruction curriculum resource set aligned to CECG's existing Ochre Education partnership format; HITP adaptation for secondary teaching contexts; an evaluation design extending the Deloitte methodology to the new domains. Framing: CECG is 5 years ahead of MACS, 5 years ahead of CSPD, and 2+ years ahead of CET in primary literacy — Phase 2 cements that lead before peers catch up to Phase 1.
opportunities.why_now: The Natalie Wexler workshop (November 2025) is an explicit public signal that CECG is actively exploring writing instruction. MACS will complete its first full year of VFI implementation in 2025 and will begin looking at secondary extension in 2026 — CECG has a narrow window to extend its lead before peers reach the same question. The Year 6–7 transition leakage to independent schools (a confirmed gap in market-solutions.json) is most acutely felt at CECG's secondary colleges — a strong secondary Catalyst programme is the clearest counter-offer to families considering Canberra Grammar or Radford.

who_cares.buyer: Patrick Ellis, Education Lead — programme custodian and the person who must build Phase 2; David de Carvalho, Executive Director — Phase 2 is the growth strategy that sustains CECG's national differentiation under his leadership
who_cares.user: Secondary teachers at CECG's Year 7–12 colleges; school principals at secondary level; Shaping Minds coaches extending coaching methodology to secondary contexts

opportunities.confidence_reasoning: Pain confirmed by multiple sources (no secondary Catalyst framework exists, Wexler workshop signal, Year 7 transition leakage). However, the opportunity is forward-looking: CECG has not yet publicly committed to Phase 2 scope or timeline, and the exact form of any engagement is not confirmed. The Wexler workshop is a strong timing signal but remains indirect evidence of commissioning intent. The main gaps: whether CECG has an internal Phase 2 roadmap in development, and whether any budget allocation has been made.

ai_capabilities.application: AI generates writing instruction lesson sequences for secondary teachers, applying Natalie Wexler's knowledge-building principles within CECG's HITP structure — providing a ready-to-use resource library for a domain where no Catalyst materials currently exist.

Student writing samples are automatically assessed against a rubric aligned to the Catalyst writing framework, giving teachers structured diagnostic information at scale rather than relying on manual holistic marking.

The system extracts knowledge-structure patterns from successful secondary writing samples to build a corpus of exemplars, enabling CECG to identify what distinguishes high-quality knowledge-building writing at each year level.

opportunities.title: Allied Health Telehealth Model for Rural and Regional Goulburn Diocese Schools
opportunities.pain_point: CECG enrols 5,670 students with disability — 24.8% of total enrolments, nearly double the estimated national Catholic school average of 10–15%. The annual report explicitly identifies allied health access (speech therapy, occupational therapy) in regional and rural schools as a named challenge that is unresolved. The 27 Goulburn diocese schools across 88,000 km² — serving communities including Adelong, Bombala, Grenfell, Lake Cargelligo, and West Wyalong — face compounded barriers: geographic isolation plus staffing shortages plus a high disability prevalence rate. CECG already operates a successful virtual delivery model (the Virtual Learning Collaborative for Year 11–12 students, led by award-winning Dr Wendy Mockler), demonstrating that the organisation has the technology and institutional capability to deliver services remotely. Extending that capability to allied health is the unresolved adjacency.
opportunities.market_evidence: The market-solutions gaps section confirms: 'No peer has publicly described a robust approach to allied health (speech therapy, OT) access for regional/remote students — a documented CECG challenge.' This is a genuine sector gap, not a problem that has been solved elsewhere. CECG's own VLC is the closest analogue in the sector — a virtual delivery model for a service previously inaccessible in rural communities. CEWA's LEADing Lights platform integrates student wellbeing data alongside academic data for principal decision-making — a related but distinct capability showing the sector is investing in integrated support infrastructure. The YMCA WA School Ready MOU for ELCs (profile.json) signals CECG is already looking for external partnerships to address early intervention gaps.
opportunities.opportunity: Design a telehealth-enabled allied health delivery model for CECG's Goulburn diocese schools — building on the technical and operational foundations of the existing VLC. Phase 1: audit of current allied health access across Goulburn diocese schools (which schools have access, at what frequency, through what model). Phase 2: telehealth delivery platform design — integrating speech and language assessment tools, OT screening instruments, and asynchronous review capability for contracted allied health professionals. Phase 3: integration with the Catalyst assessment framework, so allied health observations feed into the same evidence-based planning cycle that governs academic interventions. The framing: CECG already has 9.3% above-national-average disability enrolment as a mission commitment — this is the infrastructure investment to resource that commitment equitably across the full geographic footprint.
opportunities.why_now: Dr Wendy Mockler's 2025 Rural and Remote Education Advocate Award brought visibility to CECG's regional equity commitment and will have elevated internal conversations about scaling rural service delivery. CECG's MOU with YMCA WA School Ready for ELCs (launched 2025) shows active partner-seeking for early intervention models. The governance transition to CACGEL (incorporated company structure from January 2025) may give CECG cleaner legal footing to enter commercial partnerships for allied health delivery than the prior Commission structure permitted.

who_cares.buyer: David de Carvalho, Executive Director — this sits at the intersection of Catholic inclusion mission and procurement efficiency (telehealth is cheaper than fly-in allied health); Wendy Mockler, Virtual Learning Coordinator — already the demonstrated internal champion for rural virtual delivery
who_cares.user: Students with disability in Goulburn diocese schools; classroom teachers and support staff in rural schools who currently carry intervention load without specialist support

opportunities.confidence_reasoning: Pain is confirmed by the annual report (allied health access explicitly named as a challenge), profile.json (geographic and disability data), and market-solutions.json (sector gap confirmed — no peer has solved this). The VLC operational precedent is strong evidence that CECG has the organisational capability to execute. However, confidence is medium because: (1) no peer has validated a specific allied health telehealth model in an Australian Catholic school context, making outcome predictions less certain; (2) regulatory and funding complexity for allied health delivery across ACT and NSW jurisdictions is significant; and (3) CECG has not publicly signalled this as an imminent investment priority.

ai_capabilities.application: Speech and language assessment sessions conducted via telehealth are automatically transcribed and structured, giving allied health professionals a documented record of each session without manual note-taking during the assessment itself.

Student assessment results from speech and OT screening tools are automatically classified into intervention priority tiers, so limited specialist hours are directed to the students with highest need across the Goulburn diocese school network.

A scheduling and coordination agent manages telehealth appointment booking, allied health professional rostering across Goulburn diocese schools, and follow-up intervention reminders — removing the administrative overhead that currently falls on classroom teachers.

opportunities.title: Enrolment Intelligence Platform — Network Planning and Growth Corridor Strategy
opportunities.pain_point: CECG has no school in the Molonglo Valley — the ACT's fastest-growing residential corridor — and Stromlo Forest Anglican College opens there in February 2026. The signals file rates this as a high external risk (ext-r1, score 0.72): 'The risk is not just of share loss but of being permanently locked out of a growth corridor by a well-resourced competitor with a 5-year head start on a new student cohort.' Simultaneously, Tuggeranong (southern ACT) is declining, with 12 of 20 government schools projected to shrink by 2035, yet CECG has schools in this corridor. The system's geographic mismatch between school capacity and enrolment demand (growing north, declining south) will compound if not actively modelled and managed. CECG lacks any publicly described enrolment modelling or network planning platform.
opportunities.market_evidence: Catholic Schools Broken Bay (CSBB) is investing $300 million in capital to defend and grow enrolment share in NSW growth corridors — establishing a precinct model with K-12 pathways designed around demographic projections (market-solutions.json). While CECG's scale does not require equivalent capital, the underlying principle is the same: growth corridor competition requires proactive modelling, not reactive response. CEWA's LEADing Lights platform surfaces real-time attendance and enrolment data benchmarked against system averages, enabling principals and central office to detect enrolment trend divergence early. No Catholic system has published a forward-looking enrolment modelling and site-selection methodology combining demographic projections, competitor openings, and network optimisation — this remains a sector gap.
opportunities.opportunity: Build an enrolment intelligence platform for CECG that combines: (1) ACT and NSW demographic projection data (ABS population projections by suburb, school-age cohort modelling for Molonglo Valley, Gungahlin, and Tuggeranong corridors); (2) competitor capacity and trajectory data (Stromlo Forest Anglican College growth schedule, ACT government school openings); (3) CECG's own per-school enrolment trends by year level; and (4) an optimisation model for network planning decisions — where to invest in new capacity, where to rationalise. Phase 1 deliverable: a 10-year demand and supply model for CECG's ACT network. Phase 2: an ongoing monitoring dashboard that updates as demographic data and competitor activity changes. Framing to de Carvalho: this is the ACARA MySchool data lens applied to CECG's own network — a tool that quantifies the Molonglo Valley risk precisely enough to support a board-level capital investment decision.
opportunities.why_now: Stromlo Forest Anglican College opens February 2026 — CECG has months, not years, to quantify the exposure and begin planning a response. The CACGEL incorporation (January 2025) creates the governance structure that could authorise a capital response; a data-driven network plan is the prerequisite for any such authorisation. New ED de Carvalho commencing mid-2025 will be conducting his first full strategic planning cycle in 2026 — this is the window to place a network planning tool in front of the board before priorities are set.

who_cares.buyer: David de Carvalho, Executive Director — first strategic planning cycle; CACGEL board — capital investment decisions are now a board-level governance responsibility under the new incorporated structure
who_cares.user: Central planning and strategy team at CECG; school principals in growth and declining corridors who need forward visibility for staffing and capacity decisions

opportunities.confidence_reasoning: Pain is confirmed by multiple independent sources: signals.json (ext-r1 score 0.72 high risk, geographic mismatch signal), competitors.json (Stromlo Forest Anglican College detail), scorecard.json (geographic enrolment risk watch). The Molonglo Valley threat is clearly documented and time-sensitive. However, confidence is medium because: (1) CECG may have internal network planning capability not visible from public sources; (2) the capital decision to open a new school in Molonglo Valley depends on factors (financial reserves, land availability, ACT government approvals) that are not confirmed from available data; (3) no peer has published a validated enrolment intelligence platform model at CECG's scale that this opportunity can point to as proof.

ai_capabilities.application: The platform projects school-age population growth by suburb over a 10-year horizon, combining ABS birth cohort data and migration patterns to forecast where demand for CECG school places will be concentrated in 2028, 2030, and 2035.

Given CECG's school network, projected enrolment demand, and capital constraints, an optimisation model recommends where to invest in new capacity and where to manage decline — replacing ad hoc network decisions with a data-driven allocation framework.

An agent continuously monitors competitor school announcements, ACT government planning applications, and demographic release data to update CECG's network model when material changes occur — eliminating the need for manual environmental scanning.

opportunities.title: Secondary Transition Retention Programme — Keeping CECG Primary Families at the Year 7 Threshold
opportunities.pain_point: The ACT independent sector is now larger than the Catholic systemic sector by enrolment share (20.4% vs 19.3% as of 2025), and grew at 11x the Catholic systemic rate in 2024–2025 (2.55% vs 0.23%). The market-solutions file explicitly names 'transition from Catholic primary to Catholic secondary in the ACT' as an unresolved sector gap: 'appears to be a leakage point to independent schools for aspirational families — no peer has published a solution to this specific transition dynamic.' Canberra Grammar School is fully enrolled through 2026, taking applications for 2027 and 2028. Radford College (similar fee band to CECG) is fully enrolled at all year levels. Canberra Girls Grammar offers IB Diploma — a curriculum differentiation CECG cannot replicate. CECG's Catalyst results are strongest in primary years; the secondary value proposition is comparatively less differentiated.
opportunities.market_evidence: No peer Australian Catholic system has published a structured Year 6-to-Year 7 retention programme that demonstrably reduces leakage to independent secondary schools — this is confirmed as a sector gap. However, the analogous problem of primary-to-secondary transition exists in independent school systems, and several have used personalised communication programmes, peer-to-peer family referral schemes, and differentiated secondary programme marketing to address it. BCE's education strategy (2025–2027) treats 'transformational experiences' as the core enrolment proposition — suggesting experience-first positioning is the emerging sector response. CECG's Catalyst outcomes data (13 of top 20 ACT 'Making a Difference' schools are CECG schools) is directly relevant to aspirational families but is not currently packaged as a secondary transition proposition.
opportunities.opportunity: Design a Year 6-to-Year 7 transition retention programme for CECG — comprising: (1) an AI-assisted personalised communication journey for families of Year 5 and Year 6 students explaining CECG secondary college outcomes, Catalyst results at secondary level, and the co-curricular and faith formation offerings; (2) a data-informed identification of families most at risk of choosing independent alternatives (based on school location relative to independent school catchments, sibling patterns, and enquiry behaviour); (3) a structured Year 7 secondary open programme experience that directly addresses the differentiation gaps (IB alternative: explaining CECG's outcomes-based curriculum; co-curricular: highlighting specific programmes at each college). Phase 1: family communication audit and risk segmentation. Phase 2: personalised outreach journey design. Phase 3: Year 7 cohort analysis 12 months post-launch measuring retention rate improvement.
opportunities.why_now: Stromlo Forest Anglican College opens February 2026 — it is a K-12 pathway school, meaning it will absorb families from ELC who then never transition to CECG at any year level. This accelerates the transition leakage risk from secondary entry to primary entry. New ED de Carvalho has publicly emphasised affordability and community — both directly relevant to a retention proposition for families considering whether the CECG secondary fee premium is justified against free government alternatives or higher-fee independent alternatives.

who_cares.buyer: David de Carvalho, Executive Director — enrolment growth and community positioning are explicit strategic priorities; CECG secondary college principals — directly accountable for Year 7 intake numbers
who_cares.user: CECG's admissions and enrolment team; primary school principals who are the first point of contact for departing Year 6 families; families of current CECG Year 5 and 6 students

opportunities.confidence_reasoning: Pain confirmed by sector share data (competitors.json), market-solutions.json (explicitly named as unsolved gap), and the strength of independent competitor enrolment pipelines (CGS fully enrolled through 2026). The opportunity is real and time-sensitive. Confidence is medium rather than high because: (1) the solution involves CRM and communication programme design rather than a validated education technology deployment — execution risk is higher; (2) CECG's actual Year 6–7 transition rate is unknown from public data (no per-school data available), so the size of the problem is unquantified; (3) no peer has validated this specific intervention in an ACT context.

ai_capabilities.application: The system scores each Year 5 and Year 6 family on their likelihood of transitioning to an independent secondary school based on school location relative to independent school catchments, sibling history, and engagement signals — so the admissions team prioritises outreach where it matters most.

Personalised transition communication letters and emails are generated for each family, referencing their child's year level, the specific CECG secondary college relevant to their suburb, and tailored messaging addressing the most common reasons families consider independent alternatives.

A conversational agent on the CECG enrolment portal answers prospective Year 7 family questions about secondary colleges, curriculum, Catalyst outcomes, and fees — available outside office hours when families are typically researching school choices.

opportunities.title: Catalyst IP Formalisation and Knowledge-Sharing Platform for National and International Partners
opportunities.pain_point: CECG is the originator of Australia's most rigorously validated Catholic school improvement model. Two domestic systems have adopted Catalyst as their template (CSPD via Uplift, CET via Insight into Learning). Thirty New Zealand school leaders visited 10 CECG schools in May 2025. CECG co-presented at the Australian Catholic Education Conference (August 2025) to 200+ delegates. The signals file classifies Catalyst national adoption as a positive internal indicator with 'potential international licensing or consulting revenue stream.' Yet no formal IP protection, licensing model, or structured knowledge-sharing platform exists from available public evidence. The risk is that the model diffuses without commercial return and CECG's differentiation erodes as CET, CSPD, and eventually MACS reach comparable adoption levels.
opportunities.market_evidence: The market-solutions patterns section notes: 'Any vendor selling tools to support Uplift at CSPD should approach CECG first as the five-year-ahead version of the same deployment.' CET's adoption following a 2022 delegation visit demonstrates that the informal knowledge-sharing model already works — but transfers institutional knowledge without any structured commercial arrangement. The NZ visit in May 2025 suggests international appetite exists. No Catholic education system in Australia has formalised a consulting or licensing business for its pedagogical model — this is a genuine first-mover space. The CACGEL incorporation (registered company from January 2025) creates the legal structure that could support a commercial knowledge-sharing entity for the first time.
opportunities.opportunity: Design a structured Catalyst knowledge-sharing and consulting model for CECG — comprising: (1) IP audit to determine what CECG owns, what Ochre Education owns, and what is in the public domain within the Catalyst framework; (2) a tiered engagement model for domestic and international Catholic systems wanting to adopt Catalyst principles (from delegation visits with structured debrief to full consulting engagements to licensed resource access); (3) a digital knowledge-sharing platform for systems that have already adopted the model (CET, potentially CSPD) to share implementation data and professional learning — creating a network CECG convenes and curates. Framing: de Carvalho's background at the National Catholic Education Commission gives him direct visibility into peer system needs nationally. His NCEC role is the network through which a formalised Catalyst consulting offer would propagate most efficiently.
opportunities.why_now: De Carvalho commenced mid-2025 — his NCEC background means he personally knows the decision-makers at every Australian Catholic education system. The NZ visit in May 2025 (while he was still incoming as ED) demonstrates international demand that will intensify under his leadership. CACGEL's registered company structure (from January 2025) makes IP licensing and consulting commercially feasible in a way the prior Commission structure was not. The risk of inaction: if CECG does not formalise by 2027, MACS will reach Phase 1 adoption and become a peer, eroding CECG's thought-leadership position regardless.

who_cares.buyer: David de Carvalho, Executive Director — NCEC connections make him the natural convener of a national network; Patrick Ellis, Education Lead — the internal subject matter expert who would anchor any consulting or training engagement
who_cares.user: Catholic education systems nationally and internationally that want to adopt the Catalyst model (CET, CSPD as current adopters; MACS, CSBB, NZ Catholic Education as potential adopters)

opportunities.confidence_reasoning: The demand signals are real (CET adoption, CSPD adoption, NZ visit, ACEC presentation). However, confidence is low because: (1) no formal commercialisation plan is confirmed in public sources — this is a coverage-check opportunity, not a confirmed pain point with direct evidence; (2) the IP ownership question (CECG vs Ochre Education) is unresolved and could constrain the model significantly; (3) de Carvalho's public emphasis on faith-over-metrics may mean he prioritises knowledge-sharing as mission rather than revenue; (4) CECG's size constrains the capacity available to run a consulting function alongside its primary school system obligations. This is a genuine opportunity but one requiring deeper discovery before it can be confirmed.

ai_capabilities.application: An agent continuously monitors what peer Catholic systems are publishing about their implementation of Catalyst-adjacent programmes, surfaces insights to CECG's Catalyst team, and generates quarterly market intelligence summaries on where the sector is relative to CECG's own programme maturity.

A knowledge platform built on the Catalyst resource library enables partner systems (CET, CSPD) to query CECG's implementation evidence base — surfacing specific case studies, data, and resources relevant to their own implementation questions without requiring a CECG staff member to manually respond.

Implementation reports, Deloitte evaluation findings, and NAPLAN trend analyses are automatically condensed into structured evidence briefs suitable for use in consulting engagements with new partner systems — saving Patrick Ellis hours of manual synthesis per client.
